data.title: Vicor outlines plans for record product revenue run rate approaching $800M in 2026 amid strong demand and capacity expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Vicor Corporation (VICR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Patrizio Vinciarelli described 2026 as “a year of great opportunity for Vicor,” highlighting expectations for record bookings, revenues, and profitability, and a significant increase in utilization of the company’s first chip fab.
  • Philip Davies, Corporate VP of Global Sales & Marketing, stated, “As we exited 2025, book-to-bill ratio increased to over 1.2 in Q4 and has continued to increase in Q1. At the start of 2026, we can say that this will be a year of different challenges and greater opportunities. This should result in record bookings, revenues and profitability and significantly higher utilization of our first chip fab.”
  • CFO James Schmidt reported, “Vicor reported product revenue for the fourth quarter of $92.7 million, up 4.5% from the third quarter total of $88.7 million and up 15.3% from the fourth quarter 2024 total of $80.4 million. Royalty revenue for the fourth quarter totaled $14.5 million, a 33.1% sequential decrease from $21.7 million in the third quarter and a 7.8% decrease from $15.8 million in the fourth quarter of 2024.”
  • Vinciarelli emphasized ongoing IP enforcement, referencing a second investigation by the United States International Trade Commission into “illegal importation of power modules and computing systems, infringing Vicor's IP to nonisolated bus converters.”
  • Davies noted, “Our lead customer for VPD solutions is ramping a Gen 4 factorized power system before transitioning to a Gen 5-based solution with higher current density and performance,” with the Gen 5 transition expected to begin in the second half of 2026.

Outlook

  • Management refrained from providing specific quarterly guidance, citing unpredictability in timing and amounts related to licensing. Schmidt stated, “given that we cannot predict with certainty the timing or amounts of outcomes relating to our licensing practice, we will not provide quarterly guidance.”
  • Davies said, “As we approach high utilization of our first chip fab, we are beginning to engage customers in capacity reservation agreements to secure their supply needs.”
  • Vinciarelli described 2026 as a year of “major increase in product revenue, well above the rate of last year and at a level that we haven't enjoyed for quite some time,” and confirmed the company is targeting a run rate that could utilize 80% of fab capacity, implying product revenue could approach an $800 million run rate.

Financial Results

  • CFO Schmidt reported, “For Q4, we recorded operating income of $15.7 million, representing an operating margin of 14.6%. For the full year 2025, operating income totaled $81.8 million or 18.1% of revenue in patent litigation settlement compared to operating loss of $1.3 million or minus 0.4% of revenue in the prior year.”
  • “Net income for Q3 totaled $46.5 million GAAP diluted earnings per share was $1.01 based on a fully diluted share count of 46,297,000. For the full year 2025, net income increased to $118.6 million from $6.1 million in the prior year. In 2025, fully diluted earnings per share increased to $2.61 from $0.14 in the prior year.”
  • Cash and cash equivalents at quarter-end were $402.8 million, with Q4 operating cash flow of $15.7 million and capital expenditures of $5.5 million.
  • Q4 book-to-bill improved sequentially, coming in well above 1, and 1-year backlog increased 15.8% from the prior quarter, closing at $176.9 million.

Q&A

  • Quinn Bolton, Needham & Company: Asked about fab utilization. Vinciarelli responded, “It's a combination of demand, increasing demand on a number of fronts, not just hand computing... but also in test equipment... and other end markets.”
  • Bolton, Needham & Company: Asked for clarification on IP licensing revenue base. Schmidt confirmed, “the royalty revenue I quoted in my prepared remarks of $57.4 million in 2025 does not include patent litigation settlement.”
  • Jonathan Tanwanteng, CJS Securities: Inquired about VPD customer transitions and fab expansion. Vinciarelli explained, “the Gen 4 system is mature... the next-generation system will provide an additional layer of use of capacity as we get into next year.” He also noted, “we've made 2 offers on area where we could build a facility... No deal done yet, but I would expect that we're likely to do something on this soon.”
  • Richard Shannon, Craig-Hallum Capital Group: Queried on licensing growth and content per XPU. Vinciarelli stated, “we have 2 major licensees. We expect to have a lot more and future contribution from those 2 should become quite a bit larger.” Davies added, “the opportunity for us will be somewhere between $200 to $400 per XPU. But very much depends on... the current that XPU, the number of rails, that type of thing.”
  • Justin Clare, ROTH Capital Partners: Asked about capacity utilization and CapEx for a new fab. Vinciarelli said, “existing fab being well utilized within a year,” and estimated new facility investment at “$250 million, $300 million.”
  • Multiple analysts raised questions about capacity reservations, licensing expansion, and competitive landscape. Management indicated selective engagement and ongoing negotiations for capacity and partnerships.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was positive, focusing on growth, capacity, and licensing expansion, with congratulatory remarks on financial performance and strategic progress.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, repeatedly using phrases such as “we are very confident,” “this should result in record bookings, revenues and profitability,” and “we see a major opportunity for us to grow our licensing business considerably.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts displayed increased optimism, with attention shifting from uncertainty to executing on escalating demand and scale.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management’s language evolved from cautious optimism in Q3 to marked confidence in Q4, driven by a significant backlog increase and higher book-to-bill.
  • Guidance remained non-specific, but projections for fab utilization and product revenue run rate became more explicit, with management confirming an $800 million product revenue run rate target for 2026.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on licensing, fab utilization, and competitive differentiation, but Q4 included more questions about capacity reservation, fab expansion, and customer engagement.
  • Key metrics such as product revenue, gross margin, operating income, and backlog all improved sequentially or year-over-year, while strategic priorities shifted toward scaling capacity and enforcing IP.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited unpredictability in licensing outcome timing as a reason for withholding guidance.
  • Capacity constraints at the first fab and the timing for bringing additional capacity online were discussed as challenges.
  • The competitive landscape for VPD solutions and the need for alternative sources or partners was referenced, with Vinciarelli noting, “we need to, in effect, look at making the most out of the opportunity as opposed to limiting the scope of the opportunity by wanting to do it.”
  • Analysts pressed on customer engagement, capacity reservations, and the potential for competitive alternatives in the market.

Final Takeaway

Management underscored a transformational year ahead, with record bookings, revenues, and profitability anticipated as fab utilization ramps and licensing opportunities expand. The company is targeting a run rate that could bring product revenue close to $800 million, supported by strong backlog, capacity reservations, and ongoing strategic enforcement of intellectual property. With new capacity investments on the horizon and sustained demand across high-growth markets, Vicor’s leadership expressed confidence in delivering substantial growth while navigating evolving market and operational challenges.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: PPL outlines $23B capital investment plan and extends 6%–8% EPS growth target through 2029 amid data center demand surge
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: PPL Corporation (PPL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Vincent Sorgi, President, CEO & Director, highlighted that “we finished the year exactly where we said we would, delivering safe and reliable electricity and natural gas service to more than 3.5 million customers and achieving our stated financial targets for investors.” He reported ongoing earnings of $1.81 per share, 7.1% growth from the prior year.
  • Sorgi announced an updated business plan: “For 2026, we're issuing ongoing earnings guidance of $1.90 to $1.98 per share, with a midpoint of $1.94 per share, representing 7.2% growth from 2025. We're extending our 6% to 8% annual EPS growth target through at least 2029, expecting the EPS CAGR through 2029 to be near the top end of that range based off of our 2025 ongoing earnings.”
  • The company plans $23 billion in capital investments from 2026 through 2029, an increase from the previous $20 billion plan, targeting grid hardening, modernization, and new generation in Kentucky. “Our updated plan includes the critical investments that strengthen our networks against those more frequent and severe storms and other extreme weather impacts,” Sorgi stated.
  • Sorgi shared regulatory updates, including a Kentucky rate case approval resulting in an aggregate increase of approximately $233 million in annual electric and gas revenues and approved ROEs of 9.775% for both utilities. He also noted progress on Pennsylvania and Rhode Island rate cases, with decisions expected in mid-2026.
  • The Pennsylvania data center pipeline was described as “projects in advanced stages… now total approximately 25.2 gigawatts, up another 23% since our last quarterly update.”
  • On cost efficiency, Sorgi said, “we outperformed our O&M savings target by about $20 million, achieving approximately $170 million in run rate savings from our 2021 baseline, about a year ahead of our $175 million target for the end of 2026.”
  • Sorgi highlighted a joint venture with Blackstone to build generation for data centers: “We have not embedded any earnings contributions or CapEx from the JV in our updated business plan. However, depending on the timing... we could see earnings contributions as early as the back end of our updated planning horizon.”
  • Joe Bergstein, Executive VP & CFO, reported: “On a full year basis, our 2025 GAAP earnings were $1.59 per share compared to $1.20 per share in 2024. Excluding special items, our 2025 ongoing earnings were $1.81 per share, an improvement of $0.12 and in line with expectations.”

Outlook

  • Sorgi stated, “For 2026, we're issuing ongoing earnings guidance of $1.90 to $1.98 per share, with a midpoint of $1.94 per share, representing 7.2% growth from 2025.”
  • PPL is extending its 6% to 8% annual EPS growth target through at least 2029 and expects the EPS CAGR through 2029 to be near the top end of that range.
  • The capital investment plan projects $23 billion from 2026 through 2029.
  • Sorgi noted, “Importantly, beyond this strong base plan, we see several identifiable upside opportunities to further enhance or extend our earnings growth over time.”
  • The company modified its annual dividend growth rate target to 4% to 6% while issuing equity to fund the capital plan.

Financial Results

  • For 2025, PPL achieved ongoing earnings of $1.81 per share and GAAP earnings of $1.59 per share.
  • Bergstein detailed that Kentucky results increased by $0.09 per share, Pennsylvania by $0.04 per share, and Rhode Island decreased by $0.02 per share compared to 2024. Corporate and Other was $0.01 better than last year.
  • For the fourth quarter, Kentucky increased by $0.02 per share, Pennsylvania by $0.01, Rhode Island by $0.01, and Corporate and Other by $0.03 per share versus the same period a year ago.
  • The Board declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.285 per share, a nearly 5% increase, resulting in an annualized dividend of $1.14 per share.

Q&A

  • Shahriar Pourreza, Wells Fargo, asked for more color on the Pennsylvania rate case process. Sorgi responded, “A lot of discussion around the impact of data centers on customer affordability... the main focus of the hearing. So yes, I would say that was positive.” He added, “From an overall process standpoint, I think the case continues to advance. It's going as we would expect.”
  • Jeremy Tonet, JPMorgan, inquired about Genco contracts and JV auction participation. Sorgi replied, “I don't think we would necessarily need to wait for an earnings call now. I think that would be a significant event that we would do that off cycle for sure.” Regarding auction participation: “We're still evaluating... The special auction that PJM might be holding... perhaps, but we really need to see the final structure.”
  • Steven Fleishman, Wolfe Research, asked about technology types in alternative generation. Sorgi declined specifics but said, “these are technologies that we could get online more in kind of the '28, '29 time frame.”
  • Michael Lonegan, Barclays, questioned the size of upside investment opportunities. Bergstein said, “I'm not going to quantify that today from either an EPS or a capital perspective... ultimately, the size and timing... will depend on a number of factors.”
  • Julien Dumoulin-Smith, Jefferies, asked about JV materiality and load forecasts. Sorgi noted, “I think you'll continue -- you'll see from us ultimately some earnings contribution in the back end of this decade, more meaningful into next.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was neutral to slightly positive, with a strong focus on regulatory clarity, data center pipeline growth, the Blackstone JV, and rate case outcomes. Most questions were probing for further details or timing on strategic initiatives.
  • Management maintained a confident and disciplined tone, emphasizing execution, growth visibility, and risk management. Sorgi repeatedly affirmed, “we feel really good about the underlying strength of our filing and the investments that are in there.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence was more pronounced, especially in discussing growth opportunities and regulatory progress, while analysts’ tone remained constructive but pressed for more specificity on upside catalysts.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The updated plan now extends the 6% to 8% EPS growth target through 2029, while the prior quarter only specified through 2028.
  • Capital investment guidance increased from $20 billion over the previous period to $23 billion for 2026–2029.
  • Dividend growth target was adjusted downward to 4% to 6% as the company plans further equity issuance.
  • A new business plan highlighted more robust upside opportunities, especially related to data center growth and the Blackstone JV, which was discussed in more detail than the previous quarter.
  • The tone from management has shifted to greater confidence, supported by regulatory outcomes and visibility into demand growth, while analysts continue to seek details on execution and timing of large initiatives.

Risks and Concerns

  • Sorgi identified the industry-wide challenge: “first quartile T&D performance is trending worse overall for the industry as a result of more frequent and severe storms as well as more extreme weather events.”
  • The company faces potential risks related to the timing and magnitude of data center-driven load growth and the need for corresponding generation investment.
  • Sorgi acknowledged disappointment that the Kentucky commission did not approve the earnings sharing mechanism, leading to reassessment of future rate case timing.
  • Management is monitoring regulatory developments in Pennsylvania and Rhode Island, with outcomes pending in both jurisdictions that could impact financial results.
  • There is ongoing risk around the timing of contracts for the Blackstone JV and securing turbine supply for new generation.

Final Takeaway

PPL Corporation’s management emphasized that 2025 marked a year of delivery, with operational, financial, and strategic targets met and a new business plan extending strong growth guidance through 2029. The company highlighted robust momentum in data center-driven demand, a significant capital investment increase, and disciplined cost management, all while maintaining a focus on customer affordability and credit quality. Management underscored its confidence in the execution and long-term outlook, positioning PPL as a leader in balancing growth opportunities and risk in the evolving energy landscape.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: First Citizens BancShares said to be looking for potential transactions
data.description:
First Citizens BancShares (FCNCA) (FCNCB) is said to be seeking deals, having considered pursuing a deal for KeyCorp (KEY).

The Raleigh-based bank holding company has asked advisers for potential transactions that First Citizens can pursue, people familiar with the discussions told Bloomberg News.

The move comes as First Citizens seeks to vault over a key $250B asset threshold, which comes with increased compliance costs and stricter capital and liquidity requirements, according to the Friday report by Bloomberg.

Generally, lenders look to navigate the new rules with additional profitability and revenue that come with greater scale, the report noted.

First Citizens has reportedly initiated discussions with regulators about the possibility of a major acquisition.

data.title: Vesta outlines 10%–11% rental revenue growth for 2026 as manufacturing demand surges
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Corporación Inmobiliaria Vesta, S.A.B. de C.V. (VTMX) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Lorenzo Dominique Berho Carranza reported, "2025 was a year of disciplined execution and strategic positioning for Vesta. We strengthened our platform advance, Route 2030 on schedule and made decisive decisions, which enabled Vesta to capture what we believe will be a powerful demand cycle beginning in 2026 and accelerating into 2027." He highlighted accelerated leasing in the second half of 2025, with "roughly 1.4 million square feet in new leasing during the second half of the year compared to 0.5 million square feet during the first semester." Berho Carranza emphasized Vesta's focus on Mexico City, Guadalajara, and Monterrey, and noted a shift toward manufacturing demand, with "86% of Vesta's new leases... manufacturing-related with electronics leading this activity."
  • The CEO underscored, "We exceeded guidance with rental revenues increasing 11.8% to reach $274 million, while adjusted full year 2025 NOI margin reached 94.8% and adjusted EBITDA margin reached 84.4%. Vesta FFO totaled $174.9 million in 2025, a 9.2% year-on-year increase."
  • Berho Carranza detailed ongoing development, including "approximately $330 million in projects during the year on a cash flow basis," and said, "We ended the quarter with 800,000 square feet under construction with an estimated investment of approximately $60 million and an expected yield on cost of 9.9%."
  • CFO Juan Felipe Sottil Achuttegui stated, "Our total rental income increased to $283.2 million, while rental revenues reached $273.6 million, an 11.8% year-on-year increase and exceeding the upper end of our full year revenue guidance of 10% to 11%. Adjusted NOI margin exceeded our revised guidance of 94.5%, reaching 94.8%, while adjusted EBITDA margin was in line with our guidance at 84.4%. Vesta's FFO ended 2025 at $174.9 million, a 9.2% increase compared to $160.1 million in 2024."
  • Sottil Achuttegui added, "We ended the year with $337 million in cash and cash equivalents and total debt of $1.28 billion. Net debt-to-EBITDA was 4.4x, and our loan-to-value ratio was 28.1%."

Outlook

  • Sottil Achuttegui provided guidance: "We are expecting to increase rental revenues between 10% to 11% year-on-year, while we expect to achieve 93.5% adjusted NOI margin and 83% adjusted EBITDA margin for the full year 2026."
  • Management noted that growth assumptions are based on buildings leased up until December and stabilization of currently unoccupied buildings with a "strong pipeline" for 2026.
  • Berho Carranza explained, "We have also been able to renew leases and get mark-to-market rents... our leases are indexed to inflation. So the combination of existing leases at each anniversary indexed to inflation plus mark-to-market on certain contracts, plus our ability to lease up vacant building together with new development, all combined is part of how we forecast revenue growth and therefore, guidance."

Financial Results

  • Sottil Achuttegui stated, "Total revenues were up 17.2% year-over-year, reaching $76.4 million, primarily driven by rental income from new leases and inflationary adjustments across our rental portfolio."
  • 89.9% of Q4 2025 rental revenues were denominated in U.S. dollars, up from 88.7% in Q4 2024.
  • Adjusted net operating income increased 17.2% to $69.4 million, and adjusted NOI margin was 94.6%.
  • Adjusted EBITDA totaled $61.1 million, an 18.2% increase year-over-year, with a margin expansion to 83.3%.
  • Vesta FFO excluding current tax was $39.3 million compared to $41.1 million in Q4 2024, with the decrease attributed to higher interest expense.
  • Pretax income rose to $98.5 million compared to $81.2 million in 2024, primarily due to higher gains on revaluation of investment properties, exchange gains, and higher interest income.

Q&A

  • Juan Ponce, Banco Bradesco BBI, asked about the resilience of the development pipeline if the USMCA review moves to annual reviews and leasing confidence in Guadalajara and Queretaro. Berho Carranza replied, "We believe that Mexico has invested for many years... to establish a more integrated supply chain in North America... Guadalajara is an excellent example of how the electronics sector has evolved... we are very optimistic."
  • André Mazini, Citigroup, inquired about leasing in recent projects and the Monterrey land bank acquisition. Berho Carranza explained the Monterrey land deal was achieved with seller financing and expects successful lease-up in Queretaro and Monterrey in 2026.
  • Wilfredo Jorel Guilloty, Goldman Sachs, questioned occupancy expectations and tax expense. Sottil Achuttegui attributed the tax expense to peso appreciation and expects normalization; Berho Carranza said, "We don't give any guidance on occupancy numbers... we are confident that, that number will somehow pick up throughout the year."
  • Enrique Cantu, GBM, asked about revenue growth drivers. Sottil Achuttegui cited new leases, stabilization of unoccupied buildings, and renewals.
  • Gordon Lee, BTG Pactual, asked about rent stability amid rising vacancies. Berho Carranza noted demand returned after an early-year slowdown and sees no major risk to rents.
  • Pablo Ricalde, Itaú, asked about the mix of build-to-suit and spec-to-suit developments. Berho Carranza said both would remain balanced and "spec buildings... then turn them into somehow build-to-suit."
  • Pablo Monsivais, Barclays, questioned the impact of Nissan's potential plant sale in Aguascalientes and margin guidance. Berho Carranza said any plant acquisition should benefit the sector; Sottil Achuttegui attributed margin pressure to peso strength and cost control focus.
  • Abraham Fuentes, Santander, asked about asset recycling and dividends. Sottil Achuttegui reiterated asset recycling as integral to strategy and signaled continued dividends.
  • David Soto, Scotiabank, asked about marketing vacant buildings and leasing spreads. Berho Carranza expressed confidence in the pipeline and continued double-digit leasing spreads as leases mature.
  • Felipe Barragan, unknown firm, asked about infrastructure improvements under the new administration. Berho Carranza praised proactive engagement and inclusion of industrial parks in the national plan.
  • Additional questions covered development pace and capital allocation focus for 2026, with management signaling discipline and no major land acquisition needs this year.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on the resilience of the pipeline, occupancy, leasing spreads, and margin guidance, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone but with some skepticism over cost and macro factors.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone, emphasizing discipline, long-term planning, and optimism for 2026–2027, using phrases like "we are very optimistic" and "we are confident."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained steady, with analysts shifting from questions on recovery timing to more granular inquiries on cost and development discipline.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for rental revenue growth in 2026 (10% to 11%) is consistent with the previous quarter's outlook.
  • Management continued to focus on Route 2030, but with increased emphasis on manufacturing demand and successful land acquisition, particularly in Monterrey.
  • The Q&A shifted from questions about recovery signals to specific concerns on cost, occupancy, and cash flow discipline.
  • Key financial metrics, including rental revenues, NOI margin, and FFO, showed improvement or met guidance, while cost pressures from currency movements and interest expense became more pronounced.
  • Analysts' tone shifted from broader uncertainty to targeted concerns, while management confidence remained high, now with increased discussion on proactive capital allocation and infrastructure execution.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited foreign exchange volatility, particularly peso strength, as impacting operating costs and margins.
  • Occupancy moderated due to "normal tenant rotation and isolated shutdowns," but management sees this as non-structural and expects healthy backfill.
  • Analysts questioned the resilience of development amid potential USMCA review uncertainty and the sustainability of double-digit leasing spreads.
  • Management emphasized cost discipline, asset recycling, and close monitoring of supply pipelines as mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

Management conveyed that Vesta has entered 2026 with a robust portfolio, continued strong leasing momentum, and a disciplined capital allocation approach, underpinned by confidence in structural manufacturing demand in Mexico. With clear guidance for 10%–11% rental revenue growth and sustained margin discipline, the company sees itself well-positioned to capture the next wave of demand while maintaining operational and financial prudence.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Park Hotels outlines $580M–$610M adjusted EBITDA guidance for 2026 as portfolio transformation advances
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Park Hotels & Resorts Inc. (PK) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Thomas Baltimore opened by highlighting "meaningful progress against our strategic priorities and continued execution across the Core portfolio" in 2025, with a focus on reshaping and upgrading the portfolio, reinvesting in high-quality hotels, and pursuing high-impact redevelopment projects with ROI opportunities exceeding $1 billion. He stated, "we executed more than $120 million in Non-Core sales at a blended multiple of 21x." The company remains committed to reducing exposure to Non-Core assets by year-end and has active work streams underway for all remaining Non-Core properties.

  • Baltimore detailed the launch of Park’s sixth major redevelopment in seven years—the $108 million transformation of the Royal Palm South Beach—and significant renovations in Hawaii and New Orleans. He noted strong performance in the Core portfolio, with RevPAR up 3.2% in Q4 (5.7% excluding Royal Palm), and a 13% year-over-year increase in group revenue for Core hotels. He emphasized, "we have sold or disposed of 51 hotels for over $3 billion over the past 9 years and despite a challenging transaction environment, we have sold or disposed of 13 hotels since 2023, increasing portfolio-wide nominal RevPAR by nearly 8% and hotel-adjusted EBITDA margins by over 275 basis points."

  • Baltimore also cited the Hilton Hawaiian Village's 22% RevPAR growth in Q4 and highlighted the anticipated recovery and improved performance in Hawaii as renovations are completed. He added, "we expect it [Hawaii] to be one of the most meaningful contributors to earnings growth across the portfolio." The Royal Palm Miami project is expected to yield a 15%–20% return on invested capital and double its EBITDA to nearly $28 million once stabilized.

  • CFO and COO Sean Dell'Orto reported, "for the fourth quarter, RevPAR was approximately $182, representing a nearly 1% year-over-year increase or nearly 3% when excluding Royal Palm." Core hotel adjusted EBITDA margin expanded 230 basis points to 30%, while Non-Core margins contracted. Dell'Orto stated, "Core hotel adjusted EBITDA increased 13% or nearly $18 million over the prior year period despite an over $4 million headwind from Royal Palm being closed."

Outlook

  • Dell'Orto provided 2026 guidance: full year RevPAR growth is projected to be flat to up 2%, expense growth is expected to be in the low single digits, and adjusted EBITDA is forecast at $580 million to $610 million. Adjusted FFO per share is expected to be $1.73 to $1.89. He noted, "Q1 to be the most challenging quarter of the year due to difficult year-over-year comparisons."

  • The company does not include material benefit from World Cup demand at the Royal Palm in guidance, and any further Non-Core sales in 2026 are not embedded in the outlook. Guidance assumes refinancing of $1.4 billion in debt at a blended rate of 5.5%.

Financial Results

  • RevPAR for Q4 2025 was $182, up nearly 1% year-over-year, or nearly 3% excluding Royal Palm. Core portfolio RevPAR excluding Royal Palm reached nearly $216, 1,500 basis points higher than Non-Core. Core hotel adjusted EBITDA margin expanded to 30%, while Non-Core contracted to 10%. Core hotel adjusted EBITDA rose by nearly $18 million compared to the prior year. Full-year RevPAR declined 2% versus 2024, with hotel-adjusted EBITDA margin at 26.5% (down 130 basis points). The primary headwind was the Royal Palm renovation.

  • CapEx for 2025 totaled nearly $300 million, including $110 million in Q4. Liquidity at year-end was reported at approximately $2 billion.

Q&A

  • Bennett Rose, Citigroup: Asked about Hawaii properties' earnings trajectory and group pace amid the Honolulu convention center closure. Dell'Orto explained that about 60% of lost convention room nights were replaced by in-house and contract business, expecting RevPAR growth near the upper end of guidance and mid-single-digit EBITDA growth for Hawaiian properties. Baltimore added, "we are seeing some green shoots and certainly believe that we'll be in kind of mid-single-digit growth in terms of visitation."

  • Rose also inquired about portfolio-wide group pace. Baltimore stated, "up about 3% for the year in '26...just our core portfolio alone, we're about 4%, 4.5%" in 2027.

  • Duane Pfennigwerth, Evercore ISI: Questioned sequential RevPAR changes at Hilton Hawaiian Village and Miami's Royal Palm reopening. Dell'Orto cited a 37% group pace decline at Hawaiian Village for Q1, attributing it to the convention center closure. Baltimore expressed confidence in Royal Palm's June opening and the company's project execution: "we think, obviously, getting open, we'll be able to capture and certainly be able to capture at very attractive rates."

  • Richard Hightower, Barclays: Asked about quarterly RevPAR cadence and expense growth. Dell'Orto said Q1 would be the weakest quarter, with improvement in Q2 and Q3, and highlighted a 2%–3% OpEx guidance for the year, noting offsets to labor costs from property-level cost controls.

  • Aryeh Klein, BMO: Asked about Non-Core asset sales and potential Core sales. Baltimore emphasized, "the goal is to get as many of them, if not all of them done this year." On Royal Palm, he noted a two-year pathway to stabilization.

  • David Katz, Jefferies: Asked if 2027 could be a year for acquisitions post-dispositions. Baltimore indicated eagerness to "make that pivot from playing defense to offense" once Non-Core sales are complete.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for details on Hawaii recovery, group pace, asset sales, and timing of Miami’s reopening, signifying a neutral to slightly skeptical tone, especially regarding visibility into stabilization and macro headwinds.

  • Management maintained a consistently confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing track record and disciplined execution. Phrases like "we remain firmly committed" and "we are confident in the long-term growth opportunities for Park" were used repeatedly. During Q&A, a slightly more defensive tone emerged when addressing asset sale timelines and macro risks, with Baltimore stating, "we know what's at stake, and we're working as hard as we can."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone stayed confident, though this quarter included more explicit caution about macroeconomic and geopolitical risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from Q3’s expectation of a rebound to a more cautious flat-to-2% RevPAR growth range in Q4, with explicit mention of macro and geopolitical risks. The 2025 Q3 call focused on anticipated recovery and strategic repositioning, while Q4 prepared remarks emphasized execution, asset disposition progress, and cost discipline.

  • Analysts' focus turned more toward timing of asset sales, project disruptions, and the specifics of Hawaii and Miami stabilization. Management continued to stress the strength of the Core portfolio, but acknowledged persistent headwinds and planned portfolio streamlining.

  • Key metrics such as RevPAR and EBITDA margin showed modest improvement in the Core portfolio but softness overall due to renovations and Non-Core drag. Management confidence remained steady, with continued emphasis on long-term value creation.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited macroeconomic uncertainty, geopolitical volatility, and international inbound demand softness—particularly from Canada—as ongoing risks that could affect group bookings and RevPAR. Dell'Orto stated, "guidance remains cautious with the potential for geopolitical or macroeconomic volatility, continuing to drive uncertainty around booking decisions and impacting short-term group pickup trends and international inbound demand, particularly from Canada."

  • Renovation-related disruptions, especially at Royal Palm and Hilton Hawaiian Village, were noted as headwinds. The company has embedded conservative assumptions in guidance regarding the timing and impact of major events.

  • Asset sale timing remains uncertain, and management reiterated its commitment to completing most Non-Core sales within 2026 while acknowledging some may lag due to disputes.

Final Takeaway

Park Hotels & Resorts Inc. delivered solid operational gains in its Core portfolio in Q4 2025, driven by successful asset sales, disciplined capital allocation, and substantial progress on major redevelopment projects. The company projects adjusted EBITDA of $580 million to $610 million for 2026, with a focus on completing the transition to a 21-hotel Core portfolio and unlocking value from ongoing renovations. Management remains confident in its long-term growth strategy, while maintaining a cautious near-term outlook amid uncertainty in the macroeconomic and geopolitical environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Newmont slips despite big Q4 beat as lower production guidance prompts uncertainty
data.description:
Newmont (NEM) -2% in Friday's trading after easily beating expectations for Q4 adjusted earnings and revenues but guiding for lower production in 2026 ahead of a planned return to growth from 2027.

The world's largest gold producer posted a Q4 profit of $1.3B, or $1.19/share, down from $1.4B, or $1.24/share, in the year-earlier quarter, but adjusted EPS of $2.52 routed the $2.05 FactSet analyst consensus estimate; revenues rose more than 20% to $6.82B, while free cash flow skyrocketed to record highs of $2.8B in Q4 and $7.3B for the full year, up 72% Y/Y.

But Newmont's (NEM) Q4 beat came entirely from higher commodity prices, not operational outperformance, as the miner's realized gold price surged 59% Y/Y to $4,216/oz, which more than compensated for a 23% decline in attributable production to 1.45M oz, impacted by planned mine sequencing at Peñasquito, Ahafo South, Yanacocha, Brucejack, and Cadia; gold by-product all-in sustaining cost was $1,302/oz for the quarter.

For the full year, Newmont (NEM) said it produced 5.9M attributable gold oz, including 5.7M oz from its core portfolio, as well as 28M oz of silver and 135K tons of copper, with gold by-product AISC of $1,358/oz.

For FY 2026, Newmont (NEM) guided for attributable gold production of ~5.3M oz, ~10% below 2025 levels, including 3.9M gold oz from managed operations, with gold by-product AISC rising to $1,680/oz.

Newmont (NEM) said it plans to spend ~$1.4B in 2026 to advance development on projects including Cadia Panel Caves and Tanami Expansion 2 in Australia, and the feasibility study at the Red Chris Block cave project in Canada; the three projects were added through the $17B acquisition of Newcrest in 2023.

The company also reported gold mineral reserves of 118.2M attributable oz at the end of 2025, down from 134.1M attributable oz at the end of 2024, mainly driven by asset sales.

data.title: There won’t be direct impact on semiconductors after SCOTUS tariff ruling – analyst
data.description:
The Supreme Court ruling finding certain tariffs unconstitutional will have minimal direct impact on the semiconductor (SMH), (SOXX), (XSD) industry because most chips were already exempted from the International Emergency Economic Powers Act tariffs, according to Bernstein senior analyst Stacy Rasgon.

“With the IEEPA ones found unconstitutional, there’s not a lot of direct impact,” Rasgon said in an interview with CNBC.

The ruling represents less of a shift for semiconductors than other industries because existing trade actions were already underway.

Rasgon noted that a Section 301 case targeting semiconductor imports from China is already in place, along with an ongoing Section 232 investigation that has been pending for some time.

“Semiconductors are going to continue doing their thing,” he said.

The primary concern surrounding tariffs is not the court ruling itself but rather the potential for more unpredictable policy implementation. Rasgon warned that indirect effects could prove more significant, particularly through tariffs on end products containing chips.

“Most semiconductors actually enter the U.S. inside other things,” he explained, noting that disruptions to consumer electronics or other finished goods could ripple back to chipmakers.

Industry attention has largely shifted away from trade policy toward the trajectory of artificial intelligence and data center spending. Capital expenditure in data centers is “going through the roof,” Rasgon said, with spending levels reaching figures that have some investors concerned about sustainability.

The semiconductor industry grew 26% last year to nearly $800B and could reach $1T this year.

A notable divergence has emerged in the market, with AI compute names like NVIDIA (NVDA) flatlined over the past nine months while peripheral sectors including memory, semi-cap equipment, and optical stocks have been “ripping.”

Rasgon attributed this to supply constraints in those segments, creating what he described as “lots of interesting things” happening across the broader semiconductor landscape.

Despite investor concerns that the cycle may be nearing its peak, Rasgon expressed optimism about the near-term outlook. He noted that 2026 looks to be a “monster” year with clear order visibility extending well into 2027.

“I’m not in the camp that thinks we’re at peak just yet,” he concluded.

data.title: Pharmaceutical tariffs still in play despite Supreme Court ruling
data.description:
• Ongoing threats from President Trump to enact tariffs on pharmaceutical products imported into the United States could still become reality despite Friday's Supreme Court ruling striking down his authority to impose so-called "reciprocal" tariffs.
  • That's because the pharma tariffs were enacted as part of Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, which is not impacted by the High Court's decision. Section 232 covers products that potentially threaten national security. 
  • Trump claimed authority for the "reciprocal" tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act of 1977. The Supreme Court ruled the president does not have that ability.
  • However, many pharmaceutical companies don't have to worry about the tariff threat thanks to deals negotiated with the White House through the "most favored nation" policy. In exchange for a three-year pass on tariffs, these pharmas are providing some of their medicines at a discounted rate through the TrumpRx website and boosting manufacturing in the US.
  • These companies include Pfizer (PFE), Eli Lilly (LLY), Novo Nordisk (NVO), Merck (MRK), Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY), Amgen (AMGN), Gilead Sciences (GILD), GSK (GSK), Novartis (NVS), Sanofi (SNY), and Roche (RHHBY)

data.title: Century Aluminum outlines 630,000-ton shipment target for 2026 as Oklahoma smelter and Mt. Holly expansion advance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Century Aluminum Company (CENX) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Jesse Gary, President and CEO, highlighted Century's leadership in the American aluminum market, referencing "exciting developments on our Oklahoma smelter partnership with EGA and the redevelopment of the Hawesville site into an AI digital infrastructure campus." Gary described the Oklahoma smelter project as "the first new smelter in the U.S. in nearly 50 years," with EGA owning 60% and Century 40%, supported by a $500 million Department of Energy grant and Bechtel retained for engineering. The smelter will use EGA’s EX technology, increasing projected size to 750,000 metric tons, which Gary stated "will more than double total U.S. aluminum production."
  • The recent sale and redevelopment of the Hawesville site brought in $200 million in cash and a 6.8% interest in the completed data center, with Gary noting the potential for "returns well in excess of the initial cash payment."
  • Gary reported "excellent performance across our smelter assets in the fourth quarter," commending Sebree for a "record year for the smelter across a suite of KPIs and profitability metrics."
  • At Jamalco, Gary highlighted hurricane preparedness and the near completion of a new on-site power turbine (TG4), expected to "substantially lower the cost structure of the refinery."
  • Gary announced repairs at Grundartangi enabling restart of Line 2 by end of April, "about 6 months sooner than originally anticipated," and confirmed insurance coverage for the outage.
  • CFO Peter Trpkovski stated, "Net sales for the quarter were $634 million, a $2 million increase sequentially," and noted, "we reported net income of $1.8 million or $0.02 per share. Our adjusted net income was $128 million or $1.25 per share, excluding exceptional items."

Outlook

  • Trpkovski provided Q1 2026 adjusted EBITDA guidance of "$215 million to $235 million," attributing improvement to higher realized LME and regional premiums, with expectations for "a $70 million to $80 million increase to Q1 adjusted EBITDA when compared with Q4 levels."
  • For 2026, Century expects to ship "approximately 630,000 tons of primary aluminum," reflecting the impact of the Mt. Holly expansion and earlier-than-expected Grundartangi restart. Once all projects are complete, annualized production would reach "closer to 750,000 tons per year."
  • Century anticipates "total CapEx for the year in the range of $115 million to $125 million" and expects cash interest to decline in 2026 due to lower coupon on senior notes and a simplified capital structure.
  • Gary stated, "2026 is setting up to be a historic year for Century, and we are laser-focused on execution to benefit from the opportunities that are in front of us."

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter shipments were "approximately 140,000 tons, a decrease from the prior quarter due to the line loss in Iceland."
  • Trpkovski reported, "Adjusted EBITDA for the fourth quarter increased $70 million to $171 million."
  • Century ended Q4 with a cash balance of $134 million, reducing net debt to $421 million.
  • The company generated operating cash flow of $170 million in Q4 and received a $75 million 45X tax credit, with a $173 million receivable outstanding for additional credits.
  • The Hawesville site sale proceeds are not included in year-end cash.

Q&A

  • Nick Giles, B. Riley Securities: Asked if Q1 guidance adds back lost EBITDA from Grundartangi. Trpkovski confirmed, "That's correct. Similar to how we did on the last call."
  • Giles questioned earnings power in different price environments and capital allocation. Trpkovski detailed price sensitivities, noting an uplift from current spot prices and that "in revenue and in power combined, that's about a $75 million uplift from our midpoint if you're taking the guide of $225 million to spot."
  • Giles inquired about the Oklahoma smelter's power contract. Gary responded, "We are working on finalizing that power contract with EGA and with PSO, who is the power provider... for an investment of this size, that power contract needs to be enabling and attractive."
  • Katja Jancic, BMO: Asked about next milestones for the new smelter. Gary listed finalizing the power contract, completing engineering with Bechtel, and progressing on financing, aiming for a final investment decision in Q4 2026.
  • Jancic asked about government project-level financing; Gary indicated, "There are a number of financing options available... some of which are potentially available from the government."
  • Matthew Key, Texas Capital: Asked about Iceland outage shipment cadence. Gary explained, "Line 1... is producing about 1/3 of the overall Grundartangi volume" until Line 2 is back up by the end of July, with full utilization expected by August.
  • Key asked if the Hawesville data center stake would fund the new smelter. Gary replied, "We will be generating significant EBITDA and cash flow just from the regular operation of the business that should be more than sufficient to cover any financing needs."
  • Giles in a follow-up asked about uses of excess cash. Gary stated, "We always have the capability to pay down debt... and then if we do have cash left over, we will look at returns to shareholders."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on operational execution, capital allocation, and project timelines, with appreciation shown for strategic moves like the Hawesville sale.
  • Management’s tone was confident in both prepared remarks and Q&A, expressing certainty about market opportunities and operational progress, with repeated emphasis on execution and capital returns. Gary asserted, "We are confident that this equity stake should provide returns well in excess of the initial cash payment."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence increased, notably due to accelerated project timelines and successful asset sales. Analysts continued to probe on capital allocation and project execution but showed greater confidence in management’s guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q1 adjusted EBITDA increased versus the prior quarter, with Q4 guidance at $170 million to $180 million and Q1 now projected at $215 million to $235 million.
  • The Oklahoma smelter project advanced from discussions with potential partners and power providers to a formal partnership with EGA and Bechtel engaged for engineering.
  • The Hawesville site review concluded with a sale and future equity stake, compared to extended review and uncertainty in Q3.
  • Restart timeline for Grundartangi accelerated by six months, versus prior uncertainty pending transformer repairs.
  • Management tone shifted from cautious optimism in Q3 to heightened confidence and execution focus in Q4, reinforced by successful project milestones and a favorable market environment.
  • Analysts’ questions shifted from risk and timeline concerns to clarifications on sensitivities, capital allocation, and long-term strategy.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing supply chain constraints for transformers at Grundartangi, but confirmed insurance coverage and earlier-than-anticipated repairs.
  • Instability in Jamaica’s power grid post-hurricane led to higher costs and production outages at Jamalco, though the new on-site turbine is expected to mitigate future risks.
  • Temporary U.S. energy price spikes impacted Sebree, although management expects normalization in future quarters.
  • Analysts raised questions about power contracts for Oklahoma and the ability to achieve favorable terms, as well as the timing and value realization from the Hawesville data center stake.

Final Takeaway

Century Aluminum’s Q4 2025 call underscored a pivotal period of transformation and accelerated execution, with major milestones including a formalized Oklahoma smelter joint venture, the Hawesville site monetization, and a six-month acceleration of the Grundartangi restart. Management projects significant volume growth and improved profitability in 2026, supported by favorable aluminum market conditions and strong capital discipline, while maintaining focus on risk mitigation and value creation for shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
